title: practical english usage - Part 359
content:
What if we invite your mother next weekend and go away the week after?

’ Let's go to the beach.' ’ Supposing it rains.' 7 haven't got a tablecloth.' ’ Suppose we use a sheet.' suggestions about things that might happen.

Suppose, supposing and what if can all be used with present tenses to make 599 su  ppose      , supposing           a  nd   wha    t  if vocabulary •  602  take: time It took a long time before/until she felt comfortable in her new school.

It took us six weeks before/until we got the house clean.

It + take (+ person) + time + before/until.

5 Before/until           is  used: It takes ages to do the shopping.

It took me three hours to get home last night.

It + take (+ person) + time + infinitive 4 Preparatory        it  is  the   subject: This house will take all week to clean.

The  ferry took them two hours to unload.

object of activity + take (+ person) + infinitive 3 The  object     of  the   activity      is  the   subject: Unloading the ferry took them two hours.

The drive took me three hours. Gardening takes a lot of time.

activity + take (+ person) + time 2 The  activity      is  the   subject: They took two hours to unload the ferry.

She takes all day to get out of the bathroom.

I took three hours to get home last night.

person + take + time + infinitive 1  The  person     is  the   subject: Five structures are common We   can use take to say how  much time we  need to do something.

(     .. . are all very sympathetic.) The people in my class are all very nice / pleasant / easy to get along with.

or simpdtico.

usually mean the same as, for example, sympatyczny, sympathised sympatisk Sympathetic is a ‘ false friend' for speakers of certain languages. It does not She's always very sympathetic when people feel ill.

I'm sympathetic towards the strikers.

who  is in trouble’ .

Sympathetic usually means ‘ sharing somebody's feelings' or ‘ sorry for somebody 601 sympathetic Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 603 vocabulary’  •          taste ►    s»3 and then        so Well, I’m  thankful that’s over.

We feel very thankful that she didn’t marry him  after all.

I'm thankful that we got home before the storm started.

or at having come  through an unpleasant experience.

Thankful is used especially for feelings of relief at having avoided a danger, She wasn 't a bit grateful to me for repairing her car.

I’m  very grateful for all your help, (     Gm- very-thankful...) Grateful is the normal word for people's reactions to kindness, favours, etc.

604 thankful         and   grateful ►    ɔʒo thank      you 572 and tell       say    » ‘ Stop eating the cake.' ‘ I’ m  just tasting it to see if it’s OK.' something. Progressive forms can be used.

Another transitive use is to say that we  are using our sense of taste to find out 3 transitive       verb:   'investigate' not / can taste onion and mint in the sauce, (     f-am-testing-.. .) taste. Progressive forms are not used. We   often use can taste (►  84).

Taste can be used with an object, to say what we  perceive with our sense of 2 transitive       verb:   'perceive' Her lips tasted like wild strawberries.

The fish soup tasted mostly of garlic.

Before a noun, taste of and taste like are used.

The wine tastes Junny. (     .. .-is tasting funny. ..) not This tastes nice. What’s in it? (     ... tastes nieefy:) to say how  something tastes. Progressive forms are not used.

Taste can be used as a linking verb (►   11), followed by an adjective or noun, 1  linking     verb 603 taste       _____        ____________ ►     iɛʒ and tall       high ►    594 and talk        speak ►     109 and take        bring 606 vocabulary' •          think We thought it important that she should go home.

I thought it better to pretend that I knew nothing.

It can be used as a preparatory object (►  269) for an infinitive or clause.

They thought her fascinating. We thought him  a fool.

or noun complement.

In a very formal style, think is sometimes followed by an object and an adjective 4 think      + object     (+ to   be)   + complement Did you think to close the windows when it started raining?
